column2: Neural Machine Translation in Low Resource and Noisy Settings
column5: Neural machine translation, Low-resource neural machine translation, Data Aug- mentation, Syntactic phrase augmentation, Interactive Machine Translation xii
column6:
Abstract
Neural machine translation (NMT) has emerged as a preferred alternative to the previous main-
stream statistical machine translation (SMT) approaches largely due to its ability to produce
better translations. NMT training is often characterized as data hungry since a lot of training
data, in the order of a few million sentence pairs, is required for training. This is indeed a bot-
tleneck for the under-resourced language-pairs and domains that genuinely lack the availability
of such resources. Machine translation (MT) researchers have tried to counter the data spar-
sity problem by enriching the training data using different strategies. In our work, we propose
different data augmentation strategies to improve the translation performance in low resource
scenarios. We also explore the challenges from the translation of user generated content. Trans-
latingusergeneratedcontentposessignificanthurdleslikeunavailabilityofdomainspecificcorpus
(parallel/monolingual), noise in terms of spelling mistake, wrong syntax, code mixing etc. We
solve these problems by proposing synthetic data augmentation and noise handling techniques.
In case of low-resource NMT, when the model generates erroneous outputs, to improve the
model’sperformance,correctingthegeneratedoutputsofthemodelandagainusingthecorrected
output to update the model are also very helpful. In interactive machine translation (MT),
humantranslatorscorrecterrorsinautomatictranslationsincollaborationwiththeMTsystems,
and this is an effective way to improve productivity gain in translation. Phrase-based statistical
MT(PB-SMT)hasbeenthemainstreamapproachtoMTforthepast30years,bothinacademia
andindustry. NeuralMT(NMT),anend-to-endlearningapproachtoMT,representsthecurrent
state-of-the-art in MT research. The recent studies on interactive MT (IMT) have indicated
that NMT can significantly outperform PB-SMT. In our work, we investigate the possibility of
integrating lexical syntactic descriptions in the form of supertags and parse tree information
into the state-of-the-art NMT model, Transformer. Interactive MT (IMT) is viewed as an
effective means to increase productivity in the translation industry. In principle, IMT aims to
reduce human effort in automatic translation workflows by employing an iterative collaborative
strategy with its two most important components, the human agent and the MT engine. So use
of interactive NMT (INMT) for output sample correction can significantly reduce the overall
translation time and active learning strategy can use human corrected samples for adapting
the underlying NMT model so that in future, the model does not repeat previous errors and
xiimproves the translation quality. Active learning helps the trained models to stay updated using
the newly corrected parallel samples in an incremental fashion.
Throughoutthethesis,weexplorethetechniquestoimprovetheNMTmodel’soutputforlow-
resourcelanguagepairsandnoisydomains. Wediscussdataaugmentationapproaches, syntactic
informationinTransformer, interactiveNMT,activelearningandhandlingusergeneratedtexts.

column2: Exploring Artificial Intelligence Techniques for Peer Review and Establishing a Research Lineage
column5: Scholarly peer review, reviewer’s comments, content analysis, natural language processing (NLP), machine learning, dataset applications, review quality, and digital libraries. xiv
column6:
Abstract
In the realm of academia, peer review has a longstanding tradition of enhancing the rigor of
scientific research. It involves researchers receiving valuable feedback from their peers, allowing
them to refine their work and ensure that only the highest-quality research contributes to our
collective knowledge. However, the peer review system faces significant challenges today. The
growing number of paper submissions and publication outlets has led to a scarcity of reviewers,
making it challenging to identify domain experts and maintain review standards. To tackle these
issues to some extent, we propose the integration of artificial intelligence (AI) techniques into
the peer review process. This integration aims to enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of peer
review while upholding its quality. This thesis focuses on enhancing scholarly communication by
utilizing Artificial Intelligence (AI) techniques to improve the peer review process. The central
goals are to elevate the quality of review comments and enhance the transparency and efficiency
of peer review. In this thesis, we utilize machine learning (ML) and natural language processing
(NLP) techniques to explore and address significant issues related to scholarly peer review.
In our first contribution, we focus on leveraging the interactions within paper review texts to
predict review decisions. By employing deep neural networks guided by cross-attention mecha-
nisms, our proposed models demonstrate promising performance in predicting review decisions.
Importantly, our aim here is not to replace human reviewers but to establish a human-AI collab-
oration where AI systems analyze review texts and paper content to predict the fate of a paper.
Transitioningtooursecondcontribution, wefocusonautomaticallygeneratingrecommendations
andpredictingdecisions. Thisisaccomplishedbyanalyzingthefulltextofpaperandcorrespond-
ing review texts. We employ deep neural network models equipped with attention mechanisms,
surpassing existing benchmarks. This provides editors and program chairs with an additional
layer of confidence in the decision-making process. Additionally, we tackle the task of automati-
cally generating meta-reviews, which are concise summaries of peer reviews and interpretations
ofeditors’decisions. Byutilizingstate-of-the-artsummarizationtechniques, weassesstheperfor-
mance of various approaches in crafting informative and concise meta-reviews. We also evaluate
how well these methods preserve the essential aspects and sentiments expressed in the original
peer reviews. In our next contributing chapter, we tackle the issue of constructiveness in peer
review comments by developing a dataset called “BetterPR”. This dataset enables the automatic
estimation of the constructiveness of peer review comments, distinguishing between constructive
and non-constructive feedback. Through manual annotation and benchmarking against standard
baselines, we analyze the performance of “BetterPR”. This dataset serves as a valuable resource
for improving peer review quality.
Furthermore, we introduce “PolitePEER” a dataset created to gauge politeness intensity in
peer reviews. Recognizing the power imbalance in peer review, we aim to address rudeness and
lack of professionalism by analyzing the tone of review comments and developing a politeness
indicator. The dataset serves as a foundation for promoting politeness and professionalism in the
peer review process. Lastly, we present a benchmark dataset of peer-reviewed texts annotated
xiiiat multiple levels. By analyzing this dataset, we aim to gain a better understanding of the
reviewers’ perspectives on certain sections and aspects of the paper, and then we will be able to
use those insights to conduct further research on peer review quality as well as other downstream
challenges. A dataset such as this facilitates the computational analysis of peer reviews and
the development of automated systems for addressing issues relating to peer review quality and
transparency. These efforts contribute to the improvement of scientific publication quality by
increasing trust and transparency in scientific research validation. In our research, we rigorously
follow the scientific method and conduct studies that involve researching the process of research
itself. Our findings are documented in published papers, conveniently listed in the publications
section, providing a comprehensive overview of the core experiments and outcomes for each
chapter. As a result of our research, we have found interesting findings related to various issues,
especially those that may facilitate the integration of AI solutions into scholarly peer review
processes.
Additionally, we have identified and documented the challenges and concerns that need to
be addressed prior to implementing an AI-assisted peer review system. It is important to note
that there are numerous other AI applications in peer review that extend beyond the scope of
our current doctoral research. We encourage the research community to build upon our findings
and address the limitations inherent in the problems we have investigated.

column2: Affective Analysis of Distress Offensive Content and Personality
column5: Affective Analysis, Offensive Content Detection, Social Media Mining, Natural Language Processing
column6:
Sentiment and emotion analysis is a well-researched problem in Natural Language Processing
(NLP) with several applications. Despite many sentiment and emotion recognition approaches, researchers confront considerable hurdles, such as dealing with forensic document context, statements conveying several emotions, the ever-increasing proliferation of distress content on the Web, and so on. Furthermore, because there are no common guidelines for communicating feelings across numerous platforms, researchers have a significant problem in developing a strategy that works efficiently in all domains. In this thesis, we put our efforts towards addressing the following tasks pertaining to the field of Sentiment and Emotion analysis: (A). Emotion Recognition from Social Media Data; (B). Emotion-assisted Networks for Distress Detection from Social Media Data; (C). Emotion Recognition from Forensic Documents (especially Suicide Notes);
(D). Behavioural analysis (Interpersonal Risk factors of Suicide, Personality Subtyping) through computational modelling.
The first problem we investigate as part of this thesis is two different problems on emotion recognition from social media data. The first problem deals with emotion recognition in conversations, where we propose an approach to detect emotion in dialogues given the utterance history and grounded knowledge. Experiments show that context and external knowledge are essential for conversational emotion recognition. In the second problem, we work towards tackling the scarce availability of relevant code-mixed data and enable future researchers to contribute to this domain. We created an emotion annotated Hindi-English (Hinglish) code-mixed dataset by performing emotion annotation on the benchmark SentiMix dataset. We propose an end-to-end transformer-based multi-task framework for sentiment detection and emotion recognition that outperforms the state-of-the-art single-task and multi-task baselines by considerable margins.
In recent times, understanding people's opinion and sentiment from their online social media interactions have been the hour's need to meet various purposes, such as mental health analysis, monitoring online content, real-time trending, etc. It motivates us to solve the next objective of the thesis, in which we propose emotion-aided systems for depression and offensive content detection from social media data. We hypothesize that harvesting users' metadata information may allow for a low-profile assessment that can complement traditional services in anticipating depression and other downstream tasks. We explore this hypothesis by leveraging Twitters's rich meta-data information to detect depression and emotion among users with significant confidence via a multi-modal multi-task system. While democratizing the Internet, social media platforms can nonetheless generate conflict by allowing erroneous information to spread at an unprecedented rate. The considerable overlap among hate speech, offensive lan-guage, aggressive posts, and other correlated tasks motivates us to investigate the interrelation among these tasks through a single emotion-aware end-to-end deep neural multi-task framework for recognizing hate-offensive material and aggressive posts when learned concurrently.
The third problem we investigate as a part of this dissertation is developing several end-to-end multitask frameworks for emotion recognition from suicide notes. According

column2: Deep Learning Applications For Video Surveillance And Re Identification Systems
column5: Convolution neural networks, object detection, background modeling, video surveillance, re-identification, feature extraction, semantic hashing, image search and retrieval
column6:
Abstract
Video surveillance is a crucial part of public safety and security systems. In general, for
good video surveillance, robust systems are required for object detection, event detection,
scene understanding, person re-identification, object tracking, and image search and re-
trieval. Machine learning-based solutions for various applications have recently become
the state-of-the-art approach. This thesis provides a solution for one of the most cru-
cial applications, abandoned object detection in video surveillance systems. It explores
various deep learning model improvement techniques that can enhance the performance
of the overall framework. It provides substantial improvements to the primary building
blocks of the surveillance system. Another major concern tackled is devising techniques
for robust representation learning, and developing insight into the utilization of available
data for model training.
One of the parts of this work is the development of a complete framework for abandoned
object detection. It is also accompanied by novel solutions for re-identification and se-
mantic hashing systems. The developed abandoned object detection framework provides
a robust mechanism for owner identification and object association via the proposed novel
object association algorithm. This work also proposes re-identification approaches that
cater to the needs of video surveillance systems. In video surveillance setups, there are
multiple non-overlapping cameras capturing the same object, leading to wide deviations
in the projected images of the same object. The proposed approach achieves an effective
re-identification model, solving this issue.
To achieve real-time performance in video surveillance, it is necessary to reduce the
running time of the constituent modules. This work also leads to the development of
improved large-scale image search and retrieval models that can enhance the performance
of semantic search on images in general and video surveillance in particular. Deep se-
mantic hashing models that can provide reasonably scalable semantic search and retrieval
performance have also been developed in this work. A common challenge for all vision
applications is partial occlusion, which should be taken care of to make the system robust.
Towards solving this, the work done leads to the development of semantic hashing models
under suppressed information, particularly for face images capturing the facial symmetry
information. All the developed models have been tested on publicly available datasets,
and the quantitative and qualitative results have been provided.

column2: Machine Learning Based Predictive Modeling for Environmental Assessment and Management
column5: Airpollutants, boostingmodels, deeplearningtechniques, particleFroude number, predictive modeling, total sediment load, sensitivity analysis, and machine learn- ing algorithms. xiv
column6:
Abstract
Natural phenomena encompass a wide range of processes and events occurring in the
natural world. Predicting natural phenomena like total sediment load, hydraulic char-
acteristics (Froude number), and air pollutants is crucial because it enables informed
decision-making in environmental engineering and urban planning. They collectively in-
fluence environmental health, urban infrastructure resilience, and public well-being, pro-
viding a holistic approach to address complex urban challenges. Total sediment load in
rivers, a critical component of aquatic ecosystems, is influenced by various factors, in-
cluding land use patterns and rainfall. Changes in total sediment load can affect river
morphology and water quality. This, in turn, can influence the efficiency of sewer systems
as sedimentation impacts their functionality and maintenance requirements. Assessing
the particle Froude number (Frn) in sewer systems is crucial for understanding the flow
dynamics within urban drainage networks. Effective sewer systems are vital for mitigating
flooding and preventing water pollution. Furthermore, air pollutants, such as particulate
matter (PM2.5 & PM10), nitrogen oxide (NOx), ozone (O3), aand sulphur dioxide (SO2)
are also factors that affect human health Air quality can be influenced by releasing pol-
lutants from sediment and wastewater, leading to health and environmental concerns.
Therefore, these interconnected domains must be studied collectively to develop holistic
urban environmental management strategies. In essence, changes in one aspect can ripple
through the others, emphasizing the need for a comprehensive approach to address the
environmental challenges faced by modern cities.
Machine learning and deep learning models have become state-of-the-art methods for
predicting natural phenomena due to their ability to extract intricate patterns from vast
datasets. They enhance prediction accuracy in fields like predicting air pollutants, Froude
numberestimating,andtotalsedimentloadbyanalyzingcomplexenvironmentalvariables.
These models enable early warnings, informed decision-making, and proactive measures
to mitigate the impact of natural events, safeguarding lives and the environment. We
have developed a predictive model for analyzing air pollutants and various novel solutions
for total sediment load in rivers, particle Froude number in sewer systems. We have
developed a predictive model for three aspects of natural phenomena, i.e., total sediment
load, hydraulic characteristics (Froude number), and air pollutant dynamics, which are
crucial for prediction because they collectively contribute to the understanding of complex
environmental processes and urban infrastructure management.
Total sediment load exerts control over the river channel morphology. Due to the non-
linear and multi-dimensional behavior of the variables impacting total sediment load,
the prediction is often complex and challenging. To handle this non-linearity and multi-
dimensional behavior of total sediment load variables, we have proposed a deep neural
network (DNN) model, which provides the most accurate prediction of the sediment load.We have compared our proposed DNN model with extreme machine learning (ELM), sup-
port vector regression (SVR), linear regression (LR), and existing empirical equations.
Similarly, the hydraulic capacity of a channel is impacted due to sediment deposition in
urban drainage and sewer systems. As a result, the self-cleansing mechanism is a widely
used phenomenon in urban drainage and sewer systems. In this context, predicting par-
ticle Frn plays an important role, and we have used various machine learning techniques,
i.e., boosting techniques, standalone and ensemble techniques for predicting particle Frn
in the sewer system. For prediction of particle Frn we have compared and contrasted
empirical, standalone, boosting and ensemble machine learning techniques and show how
the ensemble techniques provide better prediction capabilities. Air pollutants are consid-
ered a major issue for human health and environmental protection. Particulate matter
(PM2.5) is considered the most hazardous pollutant since it is very easy to inhale and can
damage the respiratory systems of the human body. We propose Gated Recurrent Unit
Based Encoder-Decoder (GRU-ED) method to predict 1-hourly, 8-hourly, and 24-hourly
PM2.5 concentrations in New Delhi, India. The predictive models for air pollutants allow
individuals and healthcare providers to take precautions during periods of high pollution,
reducing the risk of health issues.
Weconductedcomprehensiveevaluationsofthedevelopedpredictivemodelsusingdatasets.
Through rigorous comparisons with previous studies, we presented both qualitative and
quantitative results. This approach allowed us to assess the performance and effectiveness
of the developed models in predicting natural phenomena, providing valuable insights for
further research and practical applications.

column2: Enhancing domain specific knowledge preservation in low resources neural machine translation
column5: Neural Machine Translation, Natural Language Processing, Low Resource Lan- guage, Domain Knowledge Preservation, Affect Translation, Gender Translation, Reinforce- ment Learning, Curriculum Learning, Multi-task Learning, Model Explainability. xiii
column6:
Abstract

 

Neural machine translation (NMT) models have recently made significant strides in maximizing
the overall translation quality of text, which typically includes factors like fluency (how natu-
ral the translated text sounds) and adequacy (how well it conveys the meaning). Despite these
advancements, effectively transferring many key higher-level semantic nuances— such as senti-
ments, emotions, gender, and others —from the source text to its translation remains a challenge
for NMT models, especially in low-resource languages. This limitation can render the obtained
translations inadequate for direct use in specific scenarios. Therefore, in this thesis, we focus on
effectively preserving such nuanced attributes of the source text in its target counterpart during
automatic translation.

Attaining such nuanced level of focus in translation within a data-constrained environment
is not feasible with conventional likelihood (LL)-based training alone. Thus, in this work, we
explore several non-conventional training paradigms, such as reinforcement learning (RL), cur-
riculum learning (CL), and multi-task learning (MTL) to prioritize affective tone preservation
in low-resource review translation task.

Additionally, we explore probing the representational knowledge of pre-trained translation
models as another research direction. Probing the linguistic knowledge of pre-trained models
allows us to selectively identify and extract salient neurons responsible for encoding specific
linguistic phenomena and to intervene in them for attribute-controlled translation.

Overall, our work contributes to the ongoing research on domain-specific translation gener-
ation and provide valuable insights into how multiple non-conventional training paradigms can
be utilized to further enhance the quality of translation models, in low-resource settings. The
proposed approaches have the potential to significantly improve the performance of NMT sys-
tems in various domain-specific tasks, making them more effective and versatile in a wide range
of downstream applications.

column2: Enhancing medical consultations with knowledge grounded question answering and dialogue generation
column5: Medical Question Answering, Knowledge Graph, Pre-trained language models, Knowl- edge Infusion, Answer generation, Natural Language Generation, Medical Dialogue Gen- eration, BERT, BART, BioBERT. xiv
column6:
Abstract

 

Poor health remains a fundamental cause of human suffering and deprivation, highlight-
ing the critical importance of improving healthcare. A key objective of the United Na-
tions (UN) Sustainable Development Goals is to ensure universal access to high-quality
healthcare, encompassing economic coverage, availability of essential services, and ac-
cess to vital vaccinations and medications. In this context, there is growing interest in
Question-Answering (QA) and Dialogue Generation within the medical domain, with po-
tential benefits including enhanced access to healthcare services, improved patient-doctor
interactions, and cost reduction through e-medicine. Recent advancements in language
modeling and natural language generation have facilitated the development of data-driven
conversation models capable of producing natural language responses directly. However,
building effective end-to-end QA and dialogue models has been a longstanding goal of
natural language research.

Medical QA systems must accurately extract concise answers from complex text, ne-
cessitating an understanding of both explicit constraints within the question's context
and implicit medical knowledge. Conversely, end-to-end dialogue systems risk devolving
into uninteresting conversations, underscoring the need for additional information such
as medical Knowledge Graphs (KG) derived from sources like the Unified Medical Lan-
guage System (UMLS) database. This thesis focuses on Enhancing Medical Consultations
through knowledge-grounded Question Answering and Dialogue Generation.

Integrating Knowledge Graphs (KG) with Language Models (LMs) is a common strat-
egy, yet effectively combining and reasoning over KG representations and language con-
text remains challenging. The thesis addresses this by proposing the MedQAKG s
tem, an end-to-end medical knowledge-aware neural QA model. This model incorporates
KG to provide precise medical information, ensuring extracted answers are reliable and
aligned with established medical facts. Challenges such as Vector Space Inconsistency
(VSD and Excessive Knowledge Information (EKI) are tackled through innovations like
the KIMedQA system, which employs attention mechanisms to identify relevant context
words with respect to KG.

Furthermore, traditional knowledge graphs often fail to capture intricate relationships
between diverse medical symptoms and their respective treatments, hindering the model’s
capacity to furnish precise and contextually pertinent responses. To overcome this, MedEx
employs First-Order Logic (FOL)-based reasoning to encode complex relationships be-
tween diseases and their solutions, enhancing contextual relevance.

Traditional QA systems typically select answers based on specific criteria like start and
end indexes or scoring functions applied to answer sentences. However, these methods are
limited to extracting information directly from the source text, making it challenging to
determine which details to include or omit within the answer span. The emergence of
large generative models has revolutionized this approach, cnabling modern QA systems
to encode both context and questions for generating responses with a deeper understand-
ing of the content, termed as abstractive question-answering systems. To address this
advancement, we introduce the KI-MAG (Knowledge-Infused Medical Abstractive Gen-
erator) model, tailored for the medical domain. By integrating relevant medical entities
into the response generation process, KI- MAG enhances answer accuracy and mitigates
data sparsity concerns.

Despite advancements, current approaches often struggle with complex logical struc-
tures in contextual information. To address this, we introduce MedLogic-AQA, leveraging
first-order logic-based rules from context and questions to produce well-grounded answers.

The next part of the thesis explores the use of structured knowledge from knowledge
graphs to enhance dialogue generation models. We release the CDialog dataset, a high-
quality multi-turn Medical Dialog dataset, and propose a neural medical dialog system
based on it.

Overall, this thesis contributes to advancing medical consultations through innovative
approaches in question answering and dialogue generation, leveraging knowledge graphs
and advanced reasoning techniques to improve healthcare outcomes.

column2: Unveiling multimodal cues and biases in internet memes
column5: Offensive Meme detection, Emotion Detection, Sentiment Prediction, Humor Recognition, Persuasiveness, Biases Identification, Biases Mitigation, Multitasking, Multimodal- ity, Multimodal Feature Fusion, Contrastive Learning, Natural Language Processing, Deep Learn- ing, Images, Text, Image xiv
column6:
Abstract

 

 

‘The emergence of social media platforms such as Twitter, Facebook, and Instagram initiated a
unique era of online communication characterized by anonymity and unrestricted self-expression.
While this has enabled greater freedom of expression, it has also given rise to the widespread
dissemination of offensive, trolling, and predatory behavior. Memes: Multimodal media forms
have gained significant popularity on these platforms. Although most memes are intended to
be humorous, some employ dark humor to propagate offensive content. These memes serve
as tools for promoting and reinforcing stercotypes, ideologies, personalities, and philosophies.
Unfortunately, due to their subtle nature, determining the underlying intentions of memes can be
challenging. This has resulted in the increasingly common use of memes for harassment, bullying,
and targeted attacks on individuals within the realm of social media. It is, therefore, crucial
to conduct a comprehensive analysis of memes to combat these issues effectively. Considering
the prevalence of code-mixed and low-resource memes on social media platforms, a thorough
examination of these memes is essential. This thesis addresses these challenges by developing
deep learning-based models to detect offensi reastic, and persuasive memes, mostly focusing
on low-resource languages like Hindi and Hindi-English code-mixed (Hinglish)

In the first chapter, we introduce the task of Offensive Meme Detection and introduce
EmoffMeme, a large-scale dataset for Hindi memes, and demonstrate the effectiveness of a multi-
tasking deep neural network. This model incorporates CLIP and Multimodal Factorized Bilinear
(MFB) pooling to identify offensive content by analyzing emotions in memes. Additionally, we
create a dataset of Hinglish memes to train models for detecting offensive memes in code-mixed
Hindi-English, emphasizing the importance of considering linguistic diversity in meme analysis.

In the second chapter, we focus on Sarcasm Detection in Hindi memes with the help of
emotions, sentiment, and humor. We propose a multitask model for sarcasm detection, leveraging
emotion and sentiment analysis to improve performance. This model is evaluated on a dataset
of Hindi memes, demonstrating its effectiveness in distinguishing between sarcastic and non-
sarcastic content. Additionally, we introduce Mu2STS, a model focused on differentiating between
sarcasm and humor in memes, showing superior performance compared to existing methods.

Moving to the third chapter, we explore the role of continuous dimensions of emotions, i.e.,
Valence and Arousal, in Identifying Humorous and Offensive Memes, proposing two
multi-task, multi-modal frameworks that outperform unimodal approaches. The first framework
is evaluated on the OffVA dataset, a new Hindi corpus annotated with offensive, valence, and
arousal labels, serving as a benchmark for future research in this area. In another framework,
we design a method to identify the intensity of humor presented in Hindi memes with the help
of valence and arousal.

In the fourth chapter, we address the task of Persuasive Meme Identification, releasing
the Persuasive_meme dataset for Hindi-English code-mixed memes. Our study includes tasks
such as persuasive meme detection, effectiveness as: ment, and identification of persuasion techniques, underscoring the importance of multimodal approaches in understanding persuasive
content in memes.

Lastly, after developing robust models for different tasks in meme analysis for English and
Hindi memes. We then attempt to Identify and Mitigate Unintended Biases presented in
such models. We develop two methods: first, to understand and mitigate political biases in clas-
sifiers trained on EmoffMeme, and second, to reduce online sexism perpetuated through memes.
Our comprehensive approach aims to measure and mitigate unintentional bias in misogynous
meme detection models, aiming to develop effective strategies to counter their harmful impact.

Throughout the thesis, we have briefly analyzed the performance of our proposed architec-
tures. We have provided a fair comparison against various baselines and state-of-the-art methods
for various performance metrics. Simultaneously, we have also carried out detailed qualitative
and quantitative evaluations of the results obtained from our proposed algorithms.

column2: Investigating multimodal analysis and fusion techniques
column5: Natural Language Processing, Deep Learning, Multimodal Sentiment Analysis, Multimodal Emotion Recognition, Multimodal Humor Analysis, Multimodal Sarcasm Detection, Multimodal Dia- logue Generation, Multitask Learning, Multimodal Fusion
column6:
Abstract

 

Multimodal affect analysis is an interdisciplinary study and has been an emerging and prominent field
in Natural Language Processing (NLP), Computer Vision (CV), and human-computer interaction (HCI).
The motivation of multimodal is to leverage the varieties of (often distinct) information from multiple
sources for building an efficient system. For example, it is a non-trivial task to detect the sentiment of a
sarcastic sentence "My neighbors are home!! it is good to wake up at 3 am in the morning." as negative
considering only the textual information. However, suppose the system has access to some other sources
of information, e.g., visual. In that case, it can easily detect the unpleasant gestures of the speaker and
would classify it with negative sentiment polarity. Similarly, for some instances, acoustic features such
as intensity, pitch, pause etc. have important roles to play in the correctness of the system. Thus, in this
thesis, we present a comprehensive overview of the recent advances in multimodal analysis, focusing
on both classification and dialogue generation tasks. We explore the challenges and opportunities in
combining multiple modalities and highlight the potential benefits of utilizing deep learning techniques
for multimodal analysis. We discuss various deep learning techniques, including Convolutional Neural
Networks (CNNs), Transformer-based models, Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), and Attention-based
models in the context of multimodal analysis. Finally, we present current research trends and open
challenges in the field, paving the way for future research in various multimodal analysis tasks, such
as sentiment, emotion, sarcasm, and humor. We also examine the impact of different modalities on
the performance of the models and discuss techniques for multimodal fusion. We have evaluated our
approaches on different publicly available multimodal datasets and compared our multimodal analysis
approaches against several baselines and state-of-the-art (SOTA) systems. The results clearly show that
multimodal analysis significantly outperforms unimodal analysis for both classification and dialogue
generation tasks.

column2: Constructing and leveraging knowledge graphs for nlp tasks in scientific domain
column5: Knowledge Graph, Information Extraction, Contribution Identification, Multi- task learning, Adapter, Scholarly article, Machine learning, Natural Language Processing, Data Preparation, Novelty Detection, Question Answering, Neuro-symbolic. xv
column6:
Abstract

 

The  world, knowledge graphs emerge as powerful tools reshaping how we navigate
and comprehend complex information. By constructing and leveraging these interconnected net-
works, a new frontier unfolds for advancing Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks. However,
the surge in scientific publications over the past few years has presented a significant challenge
for researchers. With a vast amount of information dispersed across diverse formats and sources,
scientists face an arduous task navigating this sea of data to extract valuable insights efficiently.
Organizing data and automating the extraction of vital information necessitate the development
of a robust system. Within this context, the construction of a scientific knowledge graph (SKG)
emerges as a pivotal solution. SKG specialized type of knowledge graph that captures the unique
structure and complexity of scientific information. Specifically, a scientific knowledge graph is

designed to represent entities found in research, such as concepts, methods, results, and
their interrelationships. By serving as a structured repository housing scientific knowledge, the
SKG plays a vital role in organizing and extracting essential insights from the extensive pool of
available data. In this research, our focus lies in constructing a knowledge graph from research
publications and leveraging it to address various NLP tasks, including novelty detection, ques-
tion answering, and extracting contributions from these publications. We use Machine Learning
(ML) and NLP techniques to solve these tasks.

The first contribution in the thesis is the identification of the contribution sentence from
the research article. Our work centers on an NLP-driven method designed to automatically
detect contribution sentences within research articles. We develop a multitasking deep neural
architecture capable of processing a research statement and determining whether it shows a re-
search contribution named ContriSci. To aid this main task, we establish two auxiliary tasks
within ContriSci. The first, Section Identification, predicts the section title of the research state-
ment, while the second, Citance Classification, distinguishes between citance and non-citance
sentences. These sentences are critical for understanding the contributions of a research paper
quickly. To enhance the extraction process, we utilize a structured representation known as
triplets composed of subject, predicate, and object, which allows us to break down the con-
tributions into easily analyzable components. By detecting these triplets, we can effectively

assess the richness of the contributions and relate them to the overarching goals of the research.

As part of this thesis, we investigate moving forward in the endeavor to build the knowledge
graph. We utilize the BERT-CRF model to extract phrases from contribution sentences. The
NCG phrase extraction dataset alone lacks sufficient data to train our deep learning model effec-
tively. To address this, we integrate two additional datasets—SciERC and SciClaim. The NCG
dataset, encompassing annotations for various IUs (Information Units), serves as the foundation
for sentence classification into these [Us via a BERT-based multiclass classifier. Reorganizing

the triplet training dataset into five distinct categories, each characterized by similar syntac-tic or semantic properties, enhances the efficiency and accuracy of extracting relevant triplets.
We exhaustively generate all feasible triplet combinations and employ a combination of BERT-
based classifiers and rule-based methods for their classification. Furthermore, we developed a
sequential end-to-end deep learning model for triplet extraction from contribution sentences. our
proposed pipelined-based system demonstrates state-of-the-art results, enabling the automatic
construction of a Knowledge Graph (KG) from the extracted information. This system efficiently

identifies triplets critical to building the KG, streamlining the process with high accuracy.

Over time, various datasets and methods have emerged to automate the detection of nov-
elty. However, the focus of existing textual novelty detection studies primarily centers on broad
domains like newswire, leaving a significant gap in the availability of a comprehensive dataset
tailored specifically for scientific novelty detection. By leveraging the constructed SKG, our

next research introduces a novel corpus, SciND, designed to identify

 

scientific novelty within re-
search articles through knowledge graphs. Our dataset comprises three distinct types of triples:
those essential for constructing the knowledge graph, novel triplets indicative of novelty, and
non-novel triplets. Leveraging these triples, we construct a domain-specific scientific knowledge
graph spanning seven NLP domains, extracting novel triplets from research articles published
in 2021. For non-novel articles, we utilize blog post summaries corresponding to the research
articles. Employing a feature-based novelty detection approach as a baseline, we showcase the ef-
ficacy of our proposed dataset in facilitating novelty detection within the scientific domain. The
next section of the thesis concentrates on leveraging the knowledge from the knowledge graph
to improve the performance of an NLP application, firstly enhancing the question-answering
model to the quick review research article. To address this challenge, we developed a Question
Answering (QA) system for research articles, which holds great potential to assist researchers in
reviewing the articles effectively. We use a novel neural network-based symbolic approach for
QA on scientific articles, namely Augmented SciBiDAF. By incorporating logical boolean func-
tions into the deep neural network and leveraging the BERT embeddings, we achieve substantial
performance improvements without the need for additional parameters. Furthermore, to reduce
the reliance on domain-specific training data, we leverage external knowledge from Concept Net.

To address the final problem in our thesis, we address the problem of identiing contributions

in research articles using knowledge infusion techniques. We construct a Scientific Knowledge
Graph (SKG) consisting of 3600 NLP papers to acquire factual knowledge. Our method involves
integrating factual knowledge sourced from the SKG into a pre-trained model. We divide the
KG into distinct, non-overlapping subgroups and incorporate this knowledge into the pre-trained
ability to model using adapters. In addition, we annotate a new test set to evaluate the model’

identify sentences that make significant contributions to the papers.

column2: Advancing sentiment analysis from diverse domains to multilinguality and robustness
column5: Natural Language Processing, Deep Learning, Sentiment Analysis, Aspect Based Sentiment Analysis, Intensity, Adversarial Attack, Code-mixing, Multilingual, Robustness, Model Explainability, Language Models. xiv
column6:
Abstract

 

Sentiment analysis is a well-established area in Natural Language Processing (NLP) and finds its
applications in recommendation systems, politics, curating online trends, etc. The overwhelm-
ing popularity of social media has attracted users from diverse backgrounds, cultures, etc., to
express their opinions on many social issues. Many individuals/groups also misuse the power of
social media to influence others negatively. Bilingual users with proficiency in both native and
English languages often mix them while expressing their opinions. The same phenomenon of
language mixing is observed on various online review sites. India being a multilingual country,
this phenomenon of mixing languages is very common and with the increase of Indian users on
online platforms, such mixed language content has increased drastically.

However, research on the above-mentioned socially relevant domains and low-resource lan-
guages is very scarce. Little attention has been paid to sentiment analysis in these domains or
languages at coarse or fine-grained levels. Due to resource scarcity, researchers leverage transla-
tion systems to utilize the resources of the high-resource English language to perform sentiment
analysis in low-resource languages. However, these translation systems might affect the perfor-
mance of the system due to noise induced by them. Further, the research at the more fine-grained
level in code-mixed languages is very scarce. No attempt has been made towards addressing as-
pect based sentiment analysis (ABSA) in code-mixed languages. In addition, ABSA systems
identify the polarity of a given aspect and ignore the strength/intensity of sentiment expressed
towards it. Measuring aspect sentiment intensity is of foremost importance in designing rec-
ommendation systems. Moreover, the nature of deep learning models introduces new security
risks, particularly in the form of adversarial attacks. Adversarial attacks pose a substantial threat,
exploiting vulnerabilities and causing misclassification. Thus, there is a need to investigate the
adversarial attacks in sentiment models and fortify the models against such challenges to increase
the reliability and trustworthiness in real-world applications.

We address the challenges of sentiment analysis in low-resource socially relevant domains
for English and Indian language Hindi by introducing a balanced corpus annotated with the sen-
timent classes, viz. positive, negative, and neutral. To show the effective usage of the dataset, we
build several deep learning based models and establish them as the baselines for further research
in this direction. Further, we propose two multi-task and multilingual models for sentiment
analysis of code-mixed/monolingual and English languages. Both models are tailored for multi-
tasking, transferring the knowledge between code-mixed/monolingual and English sentiment
tasks.

‘We introduce a new task to facilitate the research on code-mixed ABSA. We offer a bench-
mark setup by creating a code-mixed Hinglish (i.e., mixing of Hindi and English) dataset for
ABSA, which is annotated with aspect terms, their sentiment values (ABSA-Mix), aspect cate-
gories, and their associated sentiment values (ACSA-Mix). To demonstrate the effective usage
of the dataset, we develop several deep learning-based models for aspect term extraction and
sentiment analysis and establish them as the baselines for further research in this direction. We
solve aspect category detection (ACD) and aspect category based sentiment analysis (ACSA)
tasks (collectively referred to as ACSA-Mix tasks) in the Seq2Seq framework, where natural
language sentences are generated to represent the outputs that allow pre-trained language mod-
els to be used effectively. Further, a multilingual multi-task joint learning framework is proposed
that transfers knowledge between Hinglish (ACSA-Mix) and English (ACSA) tasks.

We introduce a novel task called aspect based sentiment intensity analysis (ABSIA). An an-
notated review corpus for ABSIA for the English language is introduced by labeling the bench-
mark SemEval ABSA restaurant dataset with the seven (7) classes in a semi-supervised way.
Further, we propose an effective technique for joint learning where ABSA is used as a sec-
ondary task to assist the primary task, i.e., ABSIA. Further, we present a defence algorithm to
enhance the robustness of code-mixed text classifiers using auditory information of words. We
establish that our novel language modeling approach results in improved robustness towards ad-
versarial attacks on code-mixed classification tasks. We also propose an attack algorithm for the
ABSA task by exploiting the model explainability technique. Our proposed method generates
adversarial samples for a given aspect, maintaining more semantic coherency. In addition, it
can be generalized to low-resource languages, which are at high risk due to resource scarcity.
We show the effectiveness of the proposed attack using automatic and human evaluation. Our
method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in perturbation ratio, success rate, and semantic
coherence.

column2: Harnessing ai for hate speech detection in social media landscapes
column5: Hate Speech Detection, ICCPR, Artificial Neural Network, Multi-Task Learning, Meta Learning, Few-Shot Learning, Multimodal Feature, Auxiliary Data, Transformer, CNN, LSTM, BERT, Intra-User, Intra-User, UNITER, CLIP, Inter Annotator Agreement, Macro-F1, Weighted-F1, AUC-ROC, Exact Match, Hamming Loss xiv
column6:
Abstract

 

 

With the advent of the internet and numerous social media platforms, citizens now have enor-
mous opportunities to express and share their opinions on various societal and political issues.
This phenomenal growth of the internet, social media networks, and messaging platforms provide
plenty of opportunities for building intelligent systems, but these are also being heavily misused
by certain groups who often disseminate offensive, racial, and hate speeches. Hate speech dete
tion involves identifying language or content that incites hatred or violence towards individuals
or groups based on characteristics like race, ethnicity, religion, or gender. The classifiers for posts
can be unimodal, analyzing text alone, or multimodal, incorporating additional data like images
or audio. Multimodal classifiers offer richer context, improving accuracy by capturing nuances
that text alone might miss. Companies play a crucial role in developing and deploying such
technology, balancing free expression with the responsibility to curb harmful content, thereby
fostering safer online environments for all users. There are five contributing chapters focusing
on unimodal and multimodal data for English, and Hindi.

The first task to be solved as part of this dissertation is hate speech detection for English lan-
guage utilizing unimodal input in the form of text. In this work, a deep multi-task learning
(MTL) framework is proposed to leverage useful information from multiple related classifica
tion tasks in order to improve the performance of the individual task. The proposed multi-task
model is based on the shared-private scheme that assigns shared and private layers to capture
s Experiments on the
five datasets show that the proposed framework attains encouraging performance in terms of
macro-F1 and weighted-F1.

The second task of this thesis is designing hate speech classifier for Hindi. In this study, Hindi
Hate Speech Dataset (HHSD) is created following a novel hierarchical fine-grained four-layer
annotation approach. The top layer separates the posts into hateful and non-hateful categories.
The second layer further categorises hateful posts into explicit hateful and implicit hateful. The
third layer is the multilabel tagging of the post into topics, such as political, religion, racism, or
sexism. The fourth layer involves the identification of the targeted named entity, either explicitly
or implicitly. Additionally, a thorough evaluation of the data annotation schema for trustwor-
thy annotation is provided. The HHSD is the largest multi-layer annotated corpora in Hindi
compared with the existing multi-layer annotated data. Experiments on the dataset using the
transformer-based approaches in multi-task learning (MTL) attain encouraging performances in
accuracy and weighted-fl score.

The third task of this dissertation is introducing a new dataset of Hindi-English code-mixed
data (HECMD), which includes 9,357 Hindi-English code-mixed posts categorised as hateful or
non-hateful. To address the hateful identification task, this study examines with (i) training
and testing using in-domain data, (ii) zero-shot transfer learning, and (iii) few-shot learning
based on meta-learning. The evaluation makes use of 22 datasets from the related domain in
the shared-features and task-specific features from five classification

English, Urdu, Bengali, and Hindi to participate in the transfer learning and meta-learning set-
ting. The high-resource English data is translated and transliterated to obtain Devanagari and
Romanized Hindi postings to augment the training set. The in-domain train-test and zero-shot
transfer learning is carried out by experimenting with ten deep neural network models, includ-
ing Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM),
Multilingual Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (M-BERT), Multilingual
Representations for Indian Languages (MuRIL), Language-Agnostic BERT Sentence Embed-
ding (LaBSE), Cross-lingual Model - Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach (XLM-
RoBERTa), Indian Language BERT (IndicBERT), A Lite BERT (ALBERT), Bidirectional and
Auto-Regressive Transformers (BART), and DistiIBERT, and the meta-learning-based methods
such as Model agnostic meta-learning (MAML) and Meta training then meta-learning (MTM).
The findings reveal the superiority of the meta learning methods in obtaining significant macro-
F1 and weighted-F1 scores.

The fourth task is three fold that leverages sentiment, emotion, and user-based information to
solve the hate speech detection for English and Hindi. The first work proposes a CNN based
MTL models to leverage information from multiple sources. The second work focusses on in-
troducing a unique model that improves hate speech identification for the English language by
utilising intra-user and inter-user-based information. The last part covers extensive experiments
conducted by leveraging transformer encodings fused with lexicon features to obtain significant
macro-F 1 and weighted-F1.

The fifth and last task is designing hate classifier for multimodal data such as meme. In this
work, a multi-task learning-based multimodal framework is designed for the detection of hateful
memes by training four hateful meme data in a jointly fashion. This MTL framework consisting
of Contrastive language-image pre-traing (CLIP), UNiversal Image-TExt Representation model
(UNITER), and BERT trained jointly to transfer common knowledge by simultaneously training
four memes data. The outcome demonstrates that the suggested method outperforms the uni-
modal and multimodal based approaches on four multilingual benchmark datasets by attaining
significant AUC-ROC, accuracy, and F1-score.

column2: Designing security techniques for outsourced data in cloud storage services
column5: Cloud storage service, Outsourced Data, Searchable Encryption, Verifiable Com- puting, Integrity Verification, Leakage Abuse Attacks, Blockchain
column6:
Abstract

 

Tn recent years, cloud computing has gained widespread adoption and recognition as a versatile
and powerful service platform. It allows users to offload their resource-intensive computations
and data to the cloud server for efficient processing. Meanwhile, users prioritize the security and
privacy of their sensitive data by encrypting it before outsourcing it to the cloud server. However,
managing and performing basic data utilization operations such as searching and computing over
these outsourced encrypted data is challenging. In addition, checking for any modification or
erasing of data and verifying the data to ensure the integrity of these encrypted data is required.
Nowadays, blockchain attracts the attention of academia and industry. Thus, it is necessary to
review and examine the potential methods to manage and perform basic data operations over
encrypted data, including investigating different threats of data leakages. This work proposes
a set of techniques, including searching and computing over encrypted data and verifying data
integrity. In addition, utilizing blockchain-based consensus mechanisms and smart contracts
in the proposed schemes to establish trust, auditability, and accountability in cloud storage
transactions helps to mitigate the risk of data breach

We propose a blockchain-assisted privacy-preserving searchable public key encryption scheme
(BASPED) using an inverted index, which supports incentives and verifies search results on the
user end. Next, we propose two search schemes over outsourced data based on symmetric search-
able encryption. The first scheme uses blockchain for multi-keyword fuzzy search over outsourced
encrypted data (BSMFS). The second scheme leverages blockchain to facilitate secure outsourced
database searches in the cloud storage (BASDB). The next work proposes a secure and efficient
scheme for verifiable computation over outsourced encrypted data (SEVCOD). It combines the
power of multivariate polynomial factorization and homomorphic encryption to enable public
verification of the cloud server’s computation results, preserving the confidentiality of both data
and results. It further improves the scheme by leveraging blockchain technology and verifying
the computing results through smart contracts (SPVPC) to achieve fairness, transparency, and
speed up the verification process. Furthermore, we propose a secure and efficient smart contract
based privacy preserving data integrity verification mechanism (SESIV) for outsourced encrypted
data with an incentive mechanism. Then, the scheme design extension accommodates the service
for multiple data owners, facilitates batch auditing, and supports data dynamics. The incentive
mechanism facilitates rewards for each 
and unauthorized modifications.
sful audit and fines players for forgery and compen-
sation to the victim. Finally, our theoretical analysis guarantees the security and robustness of
cach proposed scheme. The performance is evaluated experimentally and compared with existing
state-of-the-art mechanisms to prove the effectiveness of each proposed mechanism.

column2: Development of multiview machine learning models for disease causative agent and prognosis prediction
column5: Protease cleavage site Prediction, Breast Cancer Prognosis Prediction, Multi Modality, Multi-view Features, Octapeptide Sequence, Protein, Gene Expression, Copy Num- ber Variation, microRNA Expression, DNA Methylation, Histopathological Whole Slide Image, Machine Learning, Deep Learning. xiv
column6:
Abstract

 

 

The development of machine learning models for disease causative agent and prognosis prediction
holds significant promise in healthcare research, aiming to assist medical professionals in making
accurate predictions about disease progression. The doctor-patient ratio in India is 1:1,674, lower
than the WHO recommended ratio of 1:1,000 reveals the necessity to computerized bio-medical
Information processing using machine learning models. However, integrating diverse biomedical
data and extracting meaningful insights from biomedical literature pose significant challenges.

Motivated by this, our doctoral work explores two critical issues: HIV-1 protease cleavage
site prediction and breast cancer survival prognosis prediction.

The HIV-1 protease cleavage site prediction is crucial for addressing global public health
concerns related to human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). The HIV protease (HIV PR) is the
causative agent of acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS). For the identification of new
therapeutic targets of HIV PR, we introduce a novel majority voting ensemble framework based
on multi-objective optimization (MOO) to enhance prediction accuracy and AUC values si-
multaneously. Our framework achieves improved results by integrating outputs from diverse
classifiers chosen through MOO. Additionally, we have studied advance cleavage site predic-
tion by combining deep learning models like stacked autoencoders (SAE) and convolution neural
networks (CNN) with ensemble techniques and multi-view feature extraction. Our approach em-
ploys various feature extraction methods, including structural, physicochemical, and sequential
features, extracted using stacked autoencoder (SAE) and convolutional neural network (CNN)
architectures. These models reduce dimensionality while retaining critical information, leading
to enhanced prediction accuracy.

The second stream of work is the breast cancer prognosis prediction. Breast cancer is one of
the most often found malignancies in women. For more focused treatment and disease manage-
ment, a better prognosis for breast cancer is crucial. Several studies have already been conducted

to accomplish this. While many studies rely on single modalities such as gene expression data

to construct predictive models, the emergence of multimodal data introduces opportunities for a
deeper exploration of breast cancer’s molecular aspects. Motivated by this, we employed a multi-
modal graph convolutional network to predict human breast cancer prognosis using three input
data modalities. Subsequently, we expanded the range of modalities and conducted extensive
experiments for further investigation. Our findings highlight the beneficial impact of multi-view
data integration on predictive model performance. Additionally, we address the challenge of dy-
namic feature integration in breast cancer prognosis studies, enhancing the comprehensiveness
and accuracy of our predictive models.

Overall, our research contributes to the advancement of machine learning models for dis-
ease prognosis prediction, offering insights and methodologies to aid medical decision-making

processes and improve patient care outcomes.

ABSTRACT
High-temperature ceramic materials hold significant scientific and technological
importance owing to their wide range of applications. The properties of these materials
strongly depend on the crystal structure and morphologies, which can be modified by
altering the processing conditions. The present work examines a novel aspect of shaping
(via uniaxial pressing and pressureless sintering) in controlling and/or mitigating the
tor)
has been introduced for the first time. The consolidation factor is a type of induced strain
in grain (of the sintered ceramic bodies) due to concomitant effect of shaping and heating
which has a different origin than lattice strain. Studies have been made to illustrate the
impact of this factor on the final changes of the ceramic and allied materials systems.
Doped-ZrO , BaTiO , TiO , BaCO systems have been utilized for this study. To examine
2 3 2 3
the discrepancies (if any) of phase development due to shaping two standard modes of
experiments have been carried out throughout the thesis work. One involves pressureless
sintering of a uniaxially compacted pellet at a given temperature while for the second case,
the same starting loose powder was heat treated (i.e., calcined) under identical sintering
conditions (without any compaction treatment). Difference (if any) in the phase,
microstructure, defect, etc. (under these two cases) were investigated primarily via X-ray
diffraction (XRD), Rietveld refinement, field emission scanning electron microscopy
(FESEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Raman spectroscopy, etc.
In contrary to other dopants (e.g., MgO, Y O , Gd O ) at same level (4 mol.%), crystal
2 3 2 3
structure and microstructure of 4 mol.% calcia-doped zirconia (4CaSZ) depicted strong
dependence on the consolidation factor. 4CaSZ sintered ceramic was stabilized in fully
tetragonal phase (100%), while at the identical condition, the calcined powder had only
30% tetragonal phase (rest being monoclinic). With ionic radii size difference (of dopant
ion and Zr4+) above 30%, CaO-doped zirconia ceramics showed the effect most
prominently. The factor was also dependent on the grain boundary mobility of dopants. The
structural analysis further revealed that sintered ceramic exhibits a fully tetragonal phase
when prepared with powder calcined at a temperature of 850 °C or below. The CaO-doped
ZrO system depicted a strong dependence for size-dependent phase transition (between
2
tetragonal and the deleterious monoclinic phase) and strongly adhered to the criterion
prescribed by Garvie. A mixed size of 4CaSZ powders was also examined for this purpose.
Compared to the market-grade yttria-stabilized zirconia (3 and 4.4 mol.% Y O -doped
2 3
zirconia) system, superior properties (involving sinterability, toughness, thermal
conductivity, etc.) were observed for this mixed grade powder calcined at different
temperatures.
The benefits of the consolidation factor were also shown for undoped ceramic systems (e.g.,
TiO and BaTiO In the
2 3
absence of this consolidation factor, the undoped ceramic systems depicted many artefacts
starting from issues with phase purity (i.e., the emergence of extra/new phases), imbalance
with stoichiometry, generation of unwanted vacancies (via depletion of ions) etc.
Among allied systems (e.g., alkaline earth metal carbonates), BaCO showed this effect
3
prominently. While the phase evolutions of the calcined powders align with standard
JCPDS files, pellets exhibit peculiarities in XRD and Raman results, indicating either
strong c-axis orientation or unusual intensity ratios. An easy compressible Ba O atomic
arrangement along the C-axis paved the path for the orientation perpendicular to the (002)
plane. The discrepancy in phase evolution was explained with respect to its difference in
energy release mechanism. While the phase transformations in relation to pressure and/or
temperature is well-known, the summary of all results from the present thesis suggest that
the dependence of such phase changes with shaping/consolidation may also need relevant
attention (wherever applicable) for future scientific understanding.
